content:
Ryan, here’s what I’d do.

During Psalm 19, I’d worship with my child and, if anything, whisper something simple:
- “Listen—God’s world is talking about Him. The sky is telling the truth about His glory.”
- When we hear, “the sun… like a bridegroom… runs its course,” I’d add, “This is a song, so it uses pictures we all see. It’s true even if it’s not a science lesson—we still say ‘sunrise,’ right?”
- When we hear, “The law of the LORD is perfect,” I’d lean in: “That means every part of God’s Word is true and good for us—including Genesis. We can trust it.”

On the drive home, a short, age-appropriate talk:
- “The Bible has different kinds of writing—songs, stories, laws. Poetry uses beautiful images; history tells what happened. But all of it is true in what God means to say.”
- “Christians who love Jesus sometimes read the ‘days’ in Genesis a little differently. But all faithful Christians confess this: God made everything by His Word, out of nothing, and He called it good. We don’t change the Bible to make people like us; we let the Bible shape us because God’s Word is perfect.”
- “When Psalm 19 says the sun ‘runs,’ that’s how we speak from earth’s view. The Bible often uses everyday language without being wrong. Inerrancy means God’s Word tells the truth according to its purpose and genre.”

Then, as a dad who wants unity in truth, I’d follow up with the elder—privately and respectfully:
- Ask for clarity: “When you say, ‘read Genesis 1 as poetic for unity,’ do you mean it’s not real history, or do you mean it has literary beauty while still reporting what God actually did?”
- Share convictions humbly: “I believe Scripture is without error and that authorial intent, the flow into Genesis 2–11, the use of ‘evening and morning,’ the genealogies, and Exodus 20:11 all push me to treat Genesis 1 as real divine action, however we work out the details.”
- Appeal to shepherding: “Could we teach the church how genre works—poetry, narrative, phenomenological language—so people don’t hear ‘poetry’ as ‘not true’? And can we make space for consciences on the length of the days without asking anyone to mute their convictions?”
- Clarify “stumbling block”: unity in Scripture is unity in the truth (John 17:17; Eph. 4:13–15). Romans 14 warns against causing a brother to sin, not against holding a view you sincerely see in the text.

With my child, I’d keep modeling:
- We love God’s world and God’s Word together (Psalm 19 holds both).
- We read each passage as it was written.
- We speak the truth in love, never trimming the Bible to fit pressure, and never treating brothers and sisters with contempt.

And if, after patient conversations, the leadership requires me to call Genesis 1 “poetry” in a way that undercuts its truthfulness or historical claims, I’d keep shepherding my child to trust the Scriptures—and I’d consider whether this is the right church home, doing so quietly and charitably.

Bottom line next Sunday: delight in Psalm 19 with your 10-year-old, teach them that poetry tells the truth, and point them to this anchor—“The law of the LORD is perfect.” We can trust every word God has spoken.
